summary: Asparouhova expresses frustration at the tech industry's defensive reaction to Jonathan Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation,' which examines harmful effects of phone and social media use on young people. She argues that the destruction of attention through smartphones and social media is one of civilization's biggest threats, compromising our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile. She wrote a piece for Arena magazine exploring why tech is psychologically avoidant of the social media debate. She contends that tech's defensiveness stems from historical and psychological scars rather than genuine disagreement with the obvious harms of doomscrolling. Her core plea is that the tech industry should demonstrate leadership in helping people live peaceably with powerful technology rather than dismissing legitimate concerns as Luddism.
key_points: The tech industry's hostile reaction to Haidt's book reflects cognitive dissonance rather than genuine disagreement, since nobody actually feels good after hours of doomscrolling
The destruction of attention through smartphones and social media is one of civilization's biggest threats because it compromises the ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile
Engaging with social media criticism risks being labeled anti-tech, which keeps many in tech from acknowledging plainly obvious harms
The conversation about social media's effects is still in its early stages despite being 20+ years into the phenomenon, and tech has an opportunity to demonstrate leadership
money_quotes: Nobody feels good after scrolling on their phones for hours, and this simple observation alone seems to reasonably justify a deeper examination of social media's effects on our psychology.

The destruction of our attention – thanks, in large part, to the combination of smartphones and social media – is one of the biggest threats we face as a civilization, as it compromises our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile.
Most people are so immersed in it that they can't see how bad it is; you can't ask someone to think clearly when their core operating system is compromised.
Running on a hamster wheel is not the same as running a marathon.
I care about this topic because I love tech and all that it hopes to promise the world: a better future for everyone.

We need to look beyond the words being said, and instead understand the motivations – and scars – that make it difficult for many people in tech to acknowledge a thing that everyone else seems to find plainly obvious.
key_insight: The tech industry's refusal to seriously engage with social media's harms stems not from genuine disagreement but from psychological defensiveness, and this avoidance itself is a threat to civilization's ability to make meaningful progress.
